Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I felt like it or until something was due soon. I have access to good resources and tech, so I'd tell myself I could catch up anytime, which meant I often didn't have a real schedule. Some weeks I'd put in maybe 14 hours total, but it wasn't consistent day-to-day. I just kind of drifted through it.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'm not someone who studies at the same time every day—that's just not how I operate. When assignments were coming up, I'd cram more, but otherwise I'd let things slide. My extracurriculars also pulled my attention, so studying happened whenever I could squeeze it in between other stuff.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I got a notification about an assignment or when I suddenly remembered something was due. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pop up and I'd think, why not, it's kind of fun. But I wasn't logging in out of genuine curiosity most of the time—it was more reactive than proactive.
Q4: There were brief moments where checking the LMS became somewhat routine, especially when those gamified elements were active. Earning points or badges gave me a small reason to log in even when I wasn't super motivated. But that habit never really stuck for long. Once the novelty wore off, I'd drift back to my irregular patterns.
Q5: The gamified tasks were actually pretty useful because they made me engage when I otherwise wouldn't have bothered. Reading materials were helpful too since I'm more of a read-write learner—I'd rather read something than watch a video. Discussion posts occasionally had interesting points, but I didn't rely on them heavily.
Q6: I mostly ignored the longer videos. Watching 28 minutes of content felt like a lot when I could skim written material faster. I also didn't engage much with optional readings or supplementary resources unless they directly connected to an assignment. If it wasn't clearly necessary, I probably skipped it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was either quick and rewarding—like the gamified stuff—or directly tied to my grade. I needed to see an immediate payoff. If something felt like busywork or too abstract, I'd lose interest fast. The connection between effort and outcome had to be obvious.
Q8: Optional activities felt way easier to ignore. Required ones had this weight to them, like I knew I'd face consequences if I didn't do them. Optional stuff? I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later never came. There was no urgency, so my low motivation meant they just fell off my radar.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying outside the LMS. I'd download PDFs and read them on my tablet offline, or I'd use my own notes and sometimes external websites to clarify concepts. I have good internet and resources, so I didn't always need to be logged in to actually learn something.
Q10: There were times I'd print out lecture notes and study from those for hours, but the LMS wouldn't show any of that. Or I'd discuss concepts with friends from my extracurriculars who were in similar courses. That kind of collaborative learning happened completely off-platform but it helped me understand things.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a reading or a set of slides once, then never open them in the LMS again. I'd study from the downloaded file multiple times, but the system would only register that single access. My actual engagement with the material was way higher than my login activity suggested.
Q12: Around midterms, things got harder. I had stuff going on with my extracurriculars and my stress levels were already medium, so adding academic pressure made me want to disengage even more. I'd log in, see everything piling up, and just feel overwhelmed. Sometimes I'd close the laptop and walk away.
Q13: Fatigue hit me hard some weeks. When I was tired or stressed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—just enough to not fail. Competing responsibilities from my activities outside class definitely pulled my attention. I'd prioritize what felt more immediate or enjoyable over coursework.
Q14: Oh, for sure. There were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about other things or just zoning out. Technically I was 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't actually absorbing anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed stuff all the time, even when I knew it mattered. Part of it was low motivation—I just couldn't make myself care enough in the moment. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure anyway, which was partly true but also just an excuse. Important things got pushed until the last possible minute.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my only real motivator. Without them, I'd drift. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS, submitting things, checking requirements, making sure I didn't miss anything. My engagement was almost entirely deadline-driven rather than curiosity-driven.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but in unexpected ways. Good feedback surprised me and made me feel like maybe I could do better. Bad feedback sometimes made me withdraw more because I'd think, what's the point. It was a mixed bag—sometimes motivating, sometimes discouraging.
Q18: The structure mattered a lot. When things were clearly laid out with specific deadlines and gamified elements, I participated more. But when the course felt open-ended or self-directed, I'd lose my way. I needed external structure because I wasn't providing much internal motivation myself.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier—they gave me little wins that kept me coming back sometimes. But the sheer number of online courses I was juggling made it harder. Eight courses is a lot, and this one sometimes got lost in the shuffle. Clearer priorities would have helped.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—browsing around, clicking through things, maybe even genuinely trying to understand, but then hitting a wall when it comes to actually producing work. It's easier to passively consume than to actively create. The motivation to submit just isn't there sometimes.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. My exam score wasn't great, but I passed. A lot of my learning happened offline or through conversations that never showed up in the logs. You can understand material without constantly being logged in—the LMS doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you logged in, what you clicked, how long you stayed. But they miss the quality of that time. They can't tell if you were focused or distracted, if you were deeply thinking or just going through motions. They measure presence, not actual learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, asking questions in your head, connecting ideas. For me, real engagement was rare this semester. I mostly did what I had to do without that deeper investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from downloaded materials, or the conversations I had with friends about course concepts. It also wouldn't capture my emotional state—how surprised I was when I actually understood something, or how my mood lifted after those gamified tasks. The human side of learning is invisible to the system.
